Finally, the pre-trained LLMs are utilized to reason in 3D scenes directly through multi-view features. Notably, our approach understands the 3D scene solely through multi-view images, without requiring the input of point cloud information or additional 3D feature extraction. Through our experiments, 3DMulti-LLM achieves impressive performance and surpasses existing 3D-input-free methods by + 12.2% and + 7.1% on ScanQA and 3DMV-VQA datasets, respectively.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: Corrections to “Unbiased Estimation of Three-Dimensional Deformation From SAR Interferometry and GNSS Observations”
item.description: In the above article [1], we made a mistake in (2). We correct it as follows. \begin{align*} \left[ {\begin{array}{c} {V_{SAR}^{ASC}}\\ {V_{SAR}^{DES}}\\ 0 \end{array}} \right] \!\!\!=\!\!\! & \left[ {\begin{array}{ccc} { - \cos {{\varphi }_{ASC}}\sin {{\theta }_{ASC}}}&{\sin {{\varphi }_{ASC}}\sin {{\theta }_{ASC}}}&{\cos {{\theta }_{ASC}}}\\ { - \cos {{\varphi }_{DES}}\sin {{\theta }_{DES}}}&{\sin {{\varphi }_{DES}}\sin {{\theta }_{DES}}}&{\cos {{\theta }_{DES}}}\\ 0&1&0 \end{array}}\! \right] \\ \left[ {\begin{array}{c} {{{V}^e}}\\ {{{V}^n}}\\ {{{V}^u}} \end{array}} \right] =& \left[ {\begin{array}{ccc} {u_e^{ASC}}&{u_n^{ASC}}&{u_u^{ASC}}\\ {u_e^{DES}}&{u_n^{DES}}&{u_u^{DES}}\\ 0&1&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{c} {{{V}^e}}\\ {{{V}^n}}\\ {{{V}^u}} \end{array}} \right]. \tag{2} \end{align*}
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Compression-Oriented Video Super-Resolution
item.description: Current compressed video super-resolution methods have achieved promising performance, but they often assume that an input video is compressed under low-delay configurations. However, under random access configurations, those methods might struggle to leverage the metadata effectively due to the large variations of metadata in different compression configurations. In this work, we propose a Compression-Oriented Video Super-Resolution (COVSR) method that can address video super-resolution for both low-delay and random-access configurations. Specifically, we first introduce an efficient compression-aware propagation (ECAP) module that dynamically adjusts propagation routes in accordance with the compression configurations. Since existing methods require reconstructing frames in a frame-by-frame manner, it is difficult to achieve efficient parallelization. However, we find that by slightly relaxing sequential dependencies, our ECAP can significantly improve inference speed. Furthermore, existing methods typically perform alignment between adjacent frames or adjacent features. However, since ECAP may propagate features along non-adjacent reference routes, it introduces new challenges for accurate cross-frame feature alignment. In response, we propose a metadata-driven alignment (MDA) module that refines cross-frame motion vectors into dense, feature-level flow offsets, enabling precise alignment across temporally distant features. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our COVSR not only achieves efficient and superior super-resolution performance but also is generalizable to various compression configurations. Our code will be available at https://covsr.github.io
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: Local Dimension Enhancement Representation Learning for Skeleton-Based Action Segmentation
item.description: Most existing self-supervised learning methods for skeleton-based temporal action segmentation (TAS) fail to capture the short-term motion semantics essential for dense frame-level prediction, as they typically learn representations that are either too coarse or motion-insensitive. This issue is reflected in local dimension collapse, which highlights the limitations of current approaches and suggests directions for improvement. Specifically, to address the issue of local dimension collapse for self-supervised learning in TAS, we propose the Local Dimension Enhancement (LoDE) framework, which introduces the local effective rank (LER) as a metric to measure and a learning objective to reduce this collapse. A new fine-grained representation scale, termed a motion unit, is defined as a temporal clip of consecutive skeleton frames to model skeleton data. Centered on this representation scale, we analyze existing methods (sequence-scale and frame-scale learning) with the tool of LER and theoretically demonstrate that introducing motion unit-scale learning is essential to alleviate local dimension collapse. Inspired by our theoretical insights, we design a multi-scale semantics module that integrates frame-, sequence-, and motion unit-scale learning, with LER-based regularization to enrich local representation diversity. These designs effectively alleviate local dimension collapse and lead to significant improvements in TAS, as evidenced by LoDE’s superior performance over state-of-the-art methods on three large-scale untrimmed datasets: PKUMMD, TSU, and BABEL. Our project website is available at https://carefreesun.github.io/LoDE_TIP_2026/
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: COMMA: Coordinate-Aware Modulated Mamba Network for 3D Dispersed Vessel Segmentation
item.description: Accurate segmentation of 3D vascular structures is essential for various medical imaging applications. The dispersed nature of vascular structures leads to inherent spatial uncertainty and necessitates location awareness, yet most current 3D medical segmentation models rely on the patch-wise training strategy that usually loses this spatial context. In this study, we introduce the Coordinate-aware Modulated Mamba Network (COMMA) and contribute a manually labeled dataset of 570 cases, the largest publicly available 3D cerebrovascular dataset to date. COMMA leverages both entire and cropped patch data through global and local branches, ensuring robust and efficient spatial location awareness. Specifically, COMMA employs a channel-compressed Mamba (ccMamba) block to efficiently encode full-resolution image data, capturing long-range dependencies while optimizing computational costs. Additionally, we propose a coordinate-aware modulated (CaM) block to enhance interactions between the global and local branches, allowing the local branch to better perceive spatial information. We evaluate COMMA on six datasets, covering two imaging modalities and five types of vascular tissues. The results demonstrate COMMA’s superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods with computational efficiency, especially in segmenting small vessels. Ablation studies further highlight the importance of our proposed modules and spatial information. The code will be available at COMMA
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: CDIR: LoRA-Inspired Attention for Efficient Composite Degradation Image Restoration
item.description: Specialized image restoration methods have been extensively explored, each targeting a specific type of degradation. However, real-world images often suffer from composite degradations, prompting growing interest in unified restoration approaches. While recent unified models have shown promising results, many are hindered by high computational complexity, limiting their deployment in resource-constrained settings. Motivated by the parameter-efficient design of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), we propose an efficient attention module specifically designed for composite degradation image restoration. The proposed method adopts a dual-branch architecture, where one branch processes features at full resolution, and the other operates with reduced spatial and channel dimensions to improve efficiency. To better adapt to diverse degradation patterns, the latter branch is further divided into two sub-branches, each incorporating dynamic operations guided by local and contextual priors. These context priors are iteratively updated within each module, drawing inspiration from feedback mechanisms in reinforcement learning, thereby enabling the model to effectively perceive and handle multiple degradation types within a unified structure. Additionally, we introduce a multi-scale feed-forward network to further enhance both performance and computational efficiency. Extensive experiments on two composite degradation benchmarks demonstrate that our proposed network, CDIR, achieves state-of-the-art performance with significantly reduced complexity and fast inference speed. In addition, CDIR shows strong adaptability to various task-specific image restoration scenarios, such as dehazing, desnowing, and deraining. It also performs robustly on domain-specific applications such as ultra-high-definition (UHD), remote sensing, and medical image restoration, highlighting its versatility and practical applicability.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: ViT-UWA: Vision Transformer Underwater-Adapter for Dense Predictions Beneath the Water Surface
item.description: Vision Transformer (ViT) and its variants have witnessed a significant success in computer vision. However, their performance may degrade in underwater dense prediction tasks due to challenges like complex underwater environments, quality degradation, and light scattering in underwater images. To solve this problem, we propose the Vision Transformer Underwater-Adapter (ViT-UWA), the first detail-focused and adapted ViT backbone for underwater dense prediction tasks, without requiring task-specific pretraining. In ViT-UWA, we first introduce High-frequency Components Prior (HFCP) to add high-frequency information of underwater images to the plain ViT, which can help recover and capture lost high-frequency information of underwater images. Then, we propose a Detail Aware Module (DAM) to obtain a detail-focused multi-scale convolutional feature pyramid, which can be used in kinds of dense prediction tasks. Through the ViT-DAM Cross Fusion (VDCF), we achieve bidirectional feature cross fusion between ViT and DAM. We evaluate ViT-UWA on multiple underwater dense prediction tasks, including semantic segmentation, instance segmentation, and object detection. With only ImageNet-22K pretraining, our ViT-UWA-B yields state-of-the-art 46.4 box AP and 44.2 mask AP on USIS10K dataset, which demonstrates the superiority of our method. Our code is available at https://github.com/Linqirui/ViT-UWA
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: EG-CMFNet: An Edge-Guided Cross-Modal Fusion Network for Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
item.description: Multimodal remote sensing data often exhibit complementary information about ground objects and are widely utilized for semantic segmentation tasks. However, spatial heterogeneity and misalignment from different modalities hinder the utilization of multimodal information. To address these challenges, an edge-guided cross-modal fusion network (EG-CMFNet) is proposed for multimodal remote sensing imagery. We introduce a multimodal adaptive feature interaction (MAFI) module to adaptively interact with modal information through dual-stage spatial–channel interactions. Furthermore, during the feature fusion stage, a lightweight feature fusion (LFF) module suppresses unimodal noise to achieve efficient fusion of multimodal complementary information. Additionally, an edge auxiliary (EA) module is designed to extract boundary features precisely, mitigating confusion between semantic and edge information. Comparative experiments demonstrate that EG-CMFNet achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and robustness across three datasets of varying scales, including WHU-OPT-SAR, Dongying, and Pohang datasets. Specifically, EG-CMFNet achieves the highest mean intersection over union of 56.05%, 94.68%, and 97.51%, as well as overall accuracy (OA) of 84.47%, 94.97%, and 97.72%, respectively. The source code is available at https://github.com/masurq/EG-CMFNet
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: A Hybrid-Domain Algorithm for High-Speed High-Squint SAR Imaging With Curved Trajectory via Fifth-Order FNCS Processing
item.description: Because of the severe 2-D range-azimuth coupling (RAC) and Doppler space-variance (DSV), high-speed high-squint (HSHS) synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imaging with curved trajectory is still extremely problematic. To address this issue, a hybrid-domain algorithm via fifth-order frequency nonlinear chirp scaling (FNCS) processing is proposed in this article. The proposed algorithm comprises three modules: range focusing, azimuth compression, and geometric correction (GC). To enhance model accuracy while simplifying the coefficient representation, a fifth-order slant range model based on equivalent velocity and parameter vectorization is initially established. After the preprocessing employed to remove bulk RAC, range focusing is accomplished using a range-variant Stolt interpolation. Then, to eliminate DSV for azimuth compression, a fifth-order FNCS processing is presented based on a minimalist derivation approach. This approach derives an explicit expression for the FNCS-processed signal directly from the determined model and NCS orders to aid in the solution of undetermined parameters. Subsequently, an instantaneous projection geometry is proposed for GC, with the capability to eliminate arbitrary geometric distortions. Finally, the results of processing simulation and real SAR data demonstrate the validity and superiority of the proposed algorithm.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Multi-Task Unified Domain Incremental Learning With Domain Difference Adapters
item.description: This paper focuses on domain incremental learning (DIL) for multiple vision tasks, including object detection, instance segmentation, and image classification. DIL aims to adapt a model to new domains over time without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. Recent DIL methods append learnable prompts to input embeddings of a frozen base model to learn from new domains. However, due to prompts’ limited representation ability, they struggle to adapt the feature space to new domain data distributions. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel DIL method named Domain Difference Adapters (DD-Adapters). Through feature visualization and singular value analysis, we identify the cross-domain clustering ability of the base model and the low-rank property of domain difference. Based on these insights, our method imposes low-rank constraints on the base model to capture the principal components of domain differences, while freezing the base model to maintain its cross-domain clustering ability, thereby adapting to new domains effectively. Additionally, we introduce a prototype-guided domain selector (PDS) to dynamically select the appropriate DD-Adapters during inference, mitigating catastrophic forgetting in DIL. Extensive experimental evaluations on eight benchmark datasets demonstrate the performance superiority of the proposed method on three vision tasks, with minimal extra parameter usage.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: Dual-Branch CNN With Enhanced Bidirectional Multiscale Sparse Cross-Attention for Remote Sensing Scene Classification
item.description: Remote sensing scene classification (RSSC) remains challenging due to large intraclass diversity and high interclass similarity, while a single backbone often struggles to capture both global semantics and local structures. This article proposes a lightweight dual-branch convolutional neural network (CNN) equipped with an enhanced bidirectional multiscale sparse cross-attention (EBiMSC) module for multiscale feature fusion. The proposed network integrates two complementary branches: a streamlined ConvNeXt-Tiny branch that enhances global contextual modeling and long-range dependency capture, and a lightweight GhostNetV3 branch that captures fine-grained textures and local details, thereby reducing confusion among visually similar categories. To strengthen cross-branch complementarity, the EBiMSC module performs bidirectional multiscale sparse cross-attention (MSC) to facilitate interaction across different scales, while a parallel lightweight enhancement path supplements informative local cues. The fused features are adaptively integrated through learnable weights to obtain discriminative and consistent representations, followed by an attention-guided lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) head for classification. Despite its concise design, the model is computationally efficient, with 19.5 million parameters and 4.24 GFLOPs, making it suitable for real-world applications. Extensive experiments on four benchmark RSSC datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared with recent CNN-based, Transformer-based, and hybrid models, validating its effectiveness and efficiency.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: EP-UNet++: Advancing Landslide Monitoring Through Enhanced Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
item.description: Landslide deformation monitoring is crucial for mitigating geological hazards and ensuring public safety in disaster-prone regions. Remote sensing imagery offers unparalleled advantages in capturing large-scale, high-resolution terrain data for landslide monitoring, but its effectiveness is hindered by challenges in accurately segmenting complex terrain features and delineating boundaries. This study proposes EP-UNet++, a hybrid architecture that integrates UNet++, pyramid vision transformer (PVT), and EfficientNet through multilevel cross-model fusion, cross-resolution alignment, and 11-channel input adaptation. The framework effectively combines local and global features, substantially enhancing the accuracy and generalization of landslide remote sensing image segmentation. In addition, local interpretable model-agnostic explanations (LIMEs) analysis is employed to interpret the contribution of each input factor to the model’s predictions. Experimental results show that the EP-UNet++ achieves a mean intersection over union (mIoU) of 74.15, a mean dice coefficient (mDice) of 85.16, and a specificity of 0.979. It achieves an improvement of roughly 4% in mIoU and 2% in mDice over the second-best model, Swin-UNet. The model also exhibits faster convergence during training and robust generalization for landslide pixel detection. These results highlight the EP-UNet++ as a highly effective approach for precise and reliable landslide remote sensing image segmentation, with potential benefits for cost-effective environmental monitoring and disaster mitigation.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Learning Action Distribution Flow for Open-Set Temporal Action Segmentation
item.description: In this paper, we tackle the open-set temporal action segmentation task, which aims to identify unknown frames while ensuring accurate segmentation of known actions in the temporal domain. Existing open-set methods struggle with identifying unknown frames due to their indistinguishability against ambiguous known frames during action transitions, resulting in significant performance degradation. To address this, we propose the action distribution flow, which models transitions between action sequences to capture the inherent feature discrepancies between unknown and known frames. Specifically, our method first models the distributions of known actions using the training data, and then interpolates these distributions along the optimal transport path for consecutive actions in the testing videos. By evaluating the likelihood of testing frames against the modeled action distribution flow, our approach effectively identifies unknown frames without requiring additional training or prior knowledge of the unknown data. Extensive experiments on open-set versions of the GTEA, 50Salads, and Breakfast datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method across all evaluation metrics.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: Spatial Interpolation of DEM With Stochastic Differential Equations-Based Diffusion Model
item.description: Despite the availability of high-precision surveying technologies, digital elevation models (DEMs) may still contain missing or degraded elevation values due to complex acquisition conditions, terrain occlusions, imaging geometry effects, and subsequent data processing procedures. Spatial interpolation methods provide a technical means to recover complete DEM data from incomplete DEM data. However, traditional interpolation methods often face problems such as insufficient model expressiveness and limited interpolation accuracy. In this study, we innovatively introduce a diffusion model based on the mean-reverting stochastic differential equation (SDE) to the DEM spatial interpolation task and propose a novel data-driven interpolation framework. This method constructs a mean-reverting SDE to map high-quality DEM data to a degraded state with fixed noise, and uses the reverse-time SDE to achieve high-precision reconstruction of missing areas. We also specifically design a unique joint loss function for DEM interpolation and reconstruction, which includes content loss, perceptual loss and terrain feature loss. In addition, we propose a maximum likelihood estimation strategy to optimize the training objective, which significantly improves the training stability of the model and the reliability of the interpolation results. Experimental results show that, compared to traditional interpolation and typical deep learning-based reconstruction models, this method performs excellently in interpolating DEM data for complex terrains (such as steep slopes, gullies and ridges). In particular, it significantly reduces interpolation errors and produces terrain details that are more consistent with the actual terrain.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: RT-DETR-AMRS: Lightweight Real-Time Transformer Detection Framework for Small Remote Sensing Targets
item.description: Accurately detecting tiny, densely packed objects in remote sensing imagery remains a formidable challenge due to severe feature vanishing and complex background clutter. In this work, we present RT-DETR-AMRS, a lightweight re-design of the real-time detection transformer (RT-DETR) specifically tailored for aerial small-object scenarios. Our approach integrates three remote sensing-oriented components: C3k2-MCAttn, a dual-path residual block equipped with Monte-Carlo attention to enrich fine-scale cues; C2f-CAMixer, a lightweight channel–spatial mixer that strengthens local feature fusion without increasing architectural complexity; and DySample, a learnable, resolution-aware upsampler that preserves spatial fidelity far better than naïve interpolation. To rigorously benchmark these upgrades, we introduce TinyRS, a challenging dataset of 20 000 images comprising four representative aerial classes captured under densely cluttered conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RT-DETR-AMRS achieves 82.5% mAP50 on TinyRS, outperforming the RT-DETR-R18 baseline by 2.5 pp and surpassing state-of-the-art YOLO variants. Crucially, our model reduces parameters by 32% and FLOPs by 21% while exhibiting superior generalization on public benchmarks, such as DOTA-v2.0 and VisDrone2019, striking an optimal balance between detection accuracy and real-time efficiency.
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: AAF-Net: An Adaptive Attention Fusion Network for Landslide Change Detection
item.description: Landslide change detection is critical for disaster monitoring but is plagued by two challenges that cause existing methods to fail: 1) missed detections when postlandslide areas (e.g., landslide soil) are spectrally similar to the preexisting landscape (e.g., bare soil), as existing fusion strategies (e.g., differencing and concatenation) often face challenges in fully resolving extreme ambiguity; and 2) fragmented and incomplete segmentations, as existing decoders lack a mechanism to enforce global completeness while refining local boundaries. To solve these, we propose the adaptive attention fusion network (AAF-Net). Its uniqueness lies in two core innovations: 1) a triplet attention fusion module (TAFM), which models the full temporal triplet (prechange, postchange, and difference) simultaneously, successfully addressing the ambiguity that often severely degrades the performance of existing methods; and 2) a hierarchical attention-guided decoder (HAGD), whose unique coarse-to-fine strategy uses deep semantic guidance to establish global completeness, and then uses this as a spatial guide to refine precise local boundaries. On the GVLM-CD dataset, AAF-Net achieves a 92.11% $F1$ -score and 85.38% IoU, significantly outperforming other state-of-the-art methods while being highly efficient (5.9 GFLOPs). This demonstrates a robust solution for postdisaster landslide detection and identification.
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: Radargrammetric 3-D Reconstruction of Multiple Moving Ship Targets via Multiangle Spaceborne SAR Imagery
item.description: The increasing complexity of the marine environments imposes higher demands on the surveillance of maritime ships. Radargrammetry can extract the height information of targets from the amplitude of multiangle synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery, which provides a more specific and accurate description of the ship targets to efficiently enhance maritime surveillance capabilities. However, existing radargrammetric methods are limited to stationary targets, which cannot meet the demands for 3-D reconstruction of multiple moving ships in maritime surveillance. To address this problem, a radargrammetric 3-D reconstruction principle and a detailed reconstruction procedure for multiple moving ships via multiangle spaceborne SAR imagery are proposed in this article. First, a radargrammetric 3-D reconstruction principle for moving targets based on the multiangle moving-target projection model is proposed, and its theoretical height reconstruction accuracy is derived. Based on this principle, a multiangle spaceborne SAR image registration algorithm for moving targets is proposed. Then, a whole radargrammetric 3-D reconstruction procedure for multiple moving targets via multiangle spaceborne SAR imagery based on the proposed principle is developed. This procedure includes the following steps: Division and refocusing of received signals from multiple moving targets, estimation of targets’ velocities, multiangle spaceborne SAR image registration, and radargrammetric 3-D reconstruction. This procedure can achieve accurate 3-D reconstruction of multiple moving ships. Simulation results including both scatterer targets and electromagnetic targets verify the correctness of the proposed radargrammetric 3-D reconstruction principle and the effectiveness of the proposed reconstruction procedure.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Scattering-Aware Graph Neural Network With Transfer Learning for Few-Shot Aircraft Detection in SAR Images
item.description: Deep neural networks integrating explainable information have shown significant progress in synthetic aperture radar-automatic target recognition (SAR-ATR). Nevertheless, their underlying mechanisms and performance in few-shot object detection (FSOD) scenarios remain insufficiently explored. From a disentanglement perspective, FSOD can be decomposed into a detection task followed by a classification task. The former emphasizes discriminative features between targets and background for localization, while the latter focuses on intraclass distinctive attributes for fine-grained classification. Therefore, this article proposes a scattering-aware graph neural network (SAGN) with transfer learning for aircraft detection in SAR imagery. The framework explicitly leverages scattering-aware prior knowledge tailored for the two subtasks, comprising two core components: a salient feature generator (SFG) for localization and a structure feature injector (SFI) for classification. First, we analyze the effectiveness of applying disentangled prior knowledge in FSOD tasks. Subsequently, the SFG module is introduced for localization, consisting of a salient feature extraction module (SEM) that enhances distinctive target-background features, and a context-aware module (CAM) that captures global spatial dependencies through expanded receptive fields. For fine-grained classification, the SFI module incorporates structural and semantic target features to enable efficient representation learning under limited samples. Especially, a triplet loss based on anchor, positive, and negative topological features is designed to improve generalization in a self-supervised manner. Furthermore, a transfer learning strategy leveraging large-scale base-class data is devised to enhance few-shot adaptation. Comprehensive experiments on the SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset demonstrate that SAGN exhibits outstanding performance, with 0.507 ${mAP}_{\text{novel}}$, 0.808 ${mAP}_{\text{base}}$, and 0.722 ${mAP}_{\text{all}}$ under 30-shot settings, validating its effectiveness and superiority in FSOD tasks.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Dual-Frequency Category-Guided Recalibration Network for Remote Sensing Image Semantic Segmentation
item.description: In remote sensing images, confusion between adjacent categories commonly occurs due to similar local textures across different land-cover types, where category boundaries often exhibit gradual transitions rather than clear separations. Furthermore, intraclass variability induced by complex environmental further weakens interclass discriminability, making it difficult for conventional models to sufficiently distinguish adjacent categories. To order to tackle this challenge, this article proposes the dual-frequency category-guided recalibration network (DFCGRNet), leveraging spatial and frequency domain modeling. The DFI module separates high- and low-frequency components via a customized wavelet transform, enhancing boundary details while preserving global structural coherence. Within the FCCE module, the wavelet frequency enhancement unit adaptively strengthens boundary cues between adjacent categories through discrete wavelet modulation. The category-guided semantic recalibration module computes category centroid vectors for each category and constructs an interclass cosine similarity matrix promote adjacent category distinction. Consequently, the DFCGRNet is empowered to enhance discriminative capacity between adjacent categories. Empirical evaluations show DFCGRNet achieves mIoU scores of 85.30%, 87.67%, and 55.11% on the Vaihingen, Potsdam, and LoveDA datasets, surpassing a range of prevailing approaches in segmentation accuracy.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: NoisePO: Efficient Semantic Noise Generation and Ranking for Diffusion-Based Text-to-Image Synthesis
item.description: Diffusion-based methods have achieved remarkable success in photorealistic image generation, leveraging iterative denoising steps to improve image quality. However, multi-step denoising often suffers from error accumulation—similar to exposure bias in autoregressive models—due to suboptimal noise estimation, which can lead to degraded semantic alignment and image fidelity. To tackle the challenge of suboptimal inner latent representations in generation and improve the inner latent, this paper introduces a novel method NoisePO, an efficient semantic noise preference optimization framework. NoisePO employs a semantic noise preference optimization generative adversarial network (NPO-GAN) and noise ranking methods to search for semantically relevant noises based on textual conditions, thus eliminating undesired semantic features while emphasizing the necessary semantic ones. Specifically, NoisePO utilizes a light NPO-GAN to generate semantic noises that encourage the latent at the previous step to incorporate more semantic information from the caption. Then, light ranking models are employed to filter out low-quality noises and select the best noise. Experimental results demonstrate that NoisePO consistently outperforms the baselines across widely used frameworks, achieving notable improvements in image quality, semantic consistency, and user-specific alignment as measured by IS, FID, CLIP, and other metrics. These results indicate that NoisePO effectively enhances synthesis quality and strengthens text-image alignment.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: DI3CL: Contrastive Learning With Dynamic Instances and Contour Consistency for SAR Land-Cover Classification Foundation Model
item.description: Although significant advances have been achieved in SAR land-cover classification, recent methods remain predominantly focused on supervised learning, which relies heavily on extensive labeled datasets. This dependency not only limits scalability and generalization but also restricts adaptability to diverse application scenarios. In this paper, a general-purpose foundation model for SAR land-cover classification is developed, serving as a robust cornerstone to accelerate the development and deployment of various downstream models. Specifically, a Dynamic Instance and Contour Consistency Contrastive Learning (DI3CL) pre-training framework is presented, which incorporates a Dynamic Instance (DI) module and a Contour Consistency (CC) module. DI module enhances global contextual awareness by enforcing local consistency across different views of the same region. CC module leverages shallow feature maps to guide the model to focus on the geometric contours of SAR land-cover objects, thereby improving structural discrimination. Additionally, to enhance robustness and generalization during pre-training, a large-scale and diverse dataset named SARSense, comprising 460,532 SAR images, is constructed to enable the model to capture comprehensive and representative features. To evaluate the generalization capability of our foundation model, we conducted extensive experiments across a variety of SAR land-cover classification tasks, including SAR land-cover mapping, water detection, and road extraction. The results consistently demonstrate that the proposed DI3CL outperforms existing methods. Our code and pre-trained weights are publicly available at: https://github.com/SARpre-train/DI3CL
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: Retrieving Lake Salinity From Microwave Imagery via Physical Mechanism Models
item.description: Salinity is one of the physicochemical property parameters in natural water, and its concentration directly affects the dielectric properties of lake water. Lake salinity microwave monitoring is insufficient now due to the limitations of spatial resolution for passive microwave radiometers and correcting lake surface roughness. Here, a physical mechanism lake salinity retrieval method is proposed, using synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery and thermal imagery as input satellite data, the modified Elfouhaily spectrum, the dielectric constant model, and a small perturbation method (SPM) as forward models, and stepwise derivation of salinity from Fresnel reflection coefficients after constructing a cost function to remove roughness contributions. The proposed model withstood validation with field and station salinity and showed high accuracy and robustness (N = 288, root mean square error (RMSE) = 0.77 ppt, and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) = 25.1%). Spatiotemporal patterns of salinity in 11 typical lakes in the Inner Mongolia-Xinjiang Lake (IMXL) were mapped from 2016 to 2024 using Sentinel-1 SAR data. The result showed that average salinity in spring (3.74 ppt) and autumn (3.69 ppt) exceeded these in summer (3.67 ppt), and the spatial patterns within the terminal lake commonly exhibit continuity and homogeneity. An examination of potential drivers emphasizes the dominant role of evaporation and precipitation on interannual salinity trends in most lakes. The low radiation forcing (SSP1–2.6) is the optimal sustainable pathway for IMXL in the 21st century, with salinity gradually declining after about three decades of development, meaning improved water quality.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: An Efficient Space-Time Forward-Looking Imaging Method for Multichannel Radar via Doppler-Based Dimensionality and Rank Reduction
item.description: Recently, a multichannel radar forward-looking imaging method based on space-time reiterative super-resolution has been proposed to improve the cross-range resolution of airborne radar. In this imaging framework, constructing an accurate steering vector matrix is crucial to the imaging quality. As the beam dwell time increases, the imaging resolution improves. However, this is accompanied by the dual challenges of accurately constructing the time-domain steering vector and high computational complexity. To address the problems, an efficient space-time super-resolution (ST-SR) method based on the acceleration framework for moving platform multichannel radar is proposed. First, based on the forward-looking imaging model, motion parameters are accurately estimated directly from the data itself, thereby constructing a precise time-domain steering vector. Second, Doppler center compensation is performed on the echo of each dwell beam position. Leveraging the characteristic of an extremely narrow forward-looking Doppler spectrum, dimensionality reduction processing is performed on the data in the Doppler domain. Then, based on the low-rank property of the covariance matrix, rank reduction processing is applied, which significantly reduces the data dimensionality and computational complexity for forward-looking super-resolution imaging. Finally, a series of simulation experiments is designed to evaluate the performance of the proposed efficient imaging method, and the method is further validated via measured data processing. The imaging results show that this method can achieve lower computational complexity while maintaining imaging resolution and quality.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Instantaneous State InSAR: Estimation and Prediction for Near Real-Time Displacement Monitoring
item.description: Urban resilience and decision-making rely on continuous monitoring of key safety indicators. The increasing availability of interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) observations offers a valuable opportunity for near real-time stability monitoring, particularly in the built environment. Traditional InSAR time-series methods use batch processing of all available data at a particular moment in time to estimate static and global displacement parameters, describing the motion of the effective scatterer over the entire evaluated time period. This batch approach limits the agility of the method to adapt to a changing temporal behavior, early anomaly detection, computational efficiency, and the systematic inclusion of newly acquired SAR data. Here, we introduce a new method to capture the complex dynamic behavior of a scatterer by estimating its instantaneous state (IS) instead of using a time-invariant parametric description. The IS estimation and prediction model uses single new SAR acquisitions to provide time updates and measurement updates using a Kalman filter methodology. It imposes smoothness constraints on the displacement signal by modeling the velocity as an exponentially correlated, mean-reverting Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process, thereby enhancing the practicality of the method, and employs the normalized median amplitude dispersion as a proxy for phase quality. The results demonstrate that IS-InSAR matches the estimation quality of batch methods in non-dynamic circumstances while more effectively capturing dynamic behavior. Updating IS with single observations enables near real-time monitoring, and the explicit specification of smoothness parameters facilitates implicit phase unwrapping.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: CDSSL: A Self-Supervised Learning Framework for Cross-Domain Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
item.description: Cross-domain remote sensing semantic segmentation often experiences performance decline due to spectral distribution inconsistencies caused by variations in satellite sensor parameters. To address this issue, this letter proposes a collaborative domain adaptation framework, termed CDSSL, which integrates style transfer and self-supervised learning. The method employs an optimal transport-based style transfer approach to align visual features across datasets. An entropy-weighted self-supervised strategy is applied to guide pseudo-label selection using prediction uncertainty, thereby improving semantic consistency throughout the adaptation process. Experiments on LoveDA and GID datasets demonstrate that our framework consistently enhances segmentation performance across diverse land-cover categories in both domain transfer directions.
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: Zero-Shot Low-Light Enhancement via Coupled Retinex–Scattering Frequency Flows
item.description: The letter presents a new zero-shot low-light image enhancement framework, Retinex-Scattering Frequency Flows (RSFF), which is based on signal processing theory instead of uninterpretable deep learning. RSFF combines a partial differential equation (PDE)-based Retinex decomposition with a multi-scale wavelet-scattering frequency flow to cross-scale propagation of priors in illumination. In contrast to data-intensive supervised networks or computationally expensive diffusion models, RSFF trains physically interpretable operators in a per-image and zero-shot way without paired training examples. We give guarantees of energy conservation and stability of deformation in the scattering domain through theory. Numerous experiments on four benchmarks show that RSFF sets state-of-the-art zero-shot performances at 21.8 dB PSNR on the LOL dataset, outperforming the state-of-the-art zero-shot algorithms by 2.9 dB and matching the state-of-the-art supervised performance, yet RSFF is $160 \times$ faster than current diffusion-based methods.
item.dc:source: IEEE Signal Processing Letters - new TOC

item.title: A Double-Stage Detail Enhancement Framework for Small Object Detection in High Spatial Resolution Remote Sensing Images
item.description: Small objects in remote sensing imagery are difficult to detect due to their limited spatial extent and severe background interference. To address this challenge, a double-stage detail enhancement framework (DSDEF) is proposed to improve detection performance through two successive stages. In the first stage, remote sensing imagery is refined by a detail-aware reconstruction module (DARM), which leverages an improved diffusion-based mechanism to restore structural integrity and edge sharpness of small targets. In the second stage, a detection network built upon the DINO architecture is enhanced with custom-designed modules for attention refinement and text-guided feature modeling. Specifically, a selective channel enhancement unit and a text-semantic perception module are introduced to improve discriminative capability and attention precision in cluttered scenes. Furthermore, a semantic-guided ranktop initialization strategy leverages textual priors to guide decoder queries, enabling more accurate localization of small objects. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed DSDEF significantly improves the detection of extremely small targets. Specifically, DSDEF achieves 79.5% mean average precision on the DIOR dataset and 95.3% on NWPU VHR-10, demonstrating its effectiveness and robustness.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Multiscale Analysis of TanDEM-X InSAR Phase Measurements for Forest Structure Characterization
item.description: Assessing 3-D forest structure at relevant spatial scales is essential, as it reflects forest condition and state. Accordingly, the spatial scale of observation plays a critical role, as natural, anthropogenic, and climate-driven disturbances affect 3-D forest structure differently at different spatial scales. Multiangular 3-D synthetic aperture radar (SAR) techniques, such as SAR interferometry, provide a promising remote sensing approach for addressing 3-D forest structure by exploiting their inherent sensitivity to the underlying 3-D forest reflectivity. TanDEM-X with its high spatial resolution allows the characterization of structural heterogeneity at fine scales. One way to do this is by exploring the spatial behavior of the interferometric phase center height. In order to assess its behavior across different (spatial) scales, a multiresolution wavelet analysis of the interferometric phase center height is proposed. Spatial heterogeneity is quantified using wavelet-based parameters, such as variance and entropy. The proposed approach is validated using airborne Lidar and TanDEM-X measurements and inventory data over the temperate forest of Froschham (Traunstein). The physical significance of the derived wavelet parameters is first validated on canopy height derived from airborne Lidar and then compared with the estimates obtained from the TanDEM-X data. The effects of the interferometric coherence level and topography on the wavelet results are discussed.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Multi-Step Adaptive Deconvolution via Multi-View for Image Enhancement in Scattering Media
item.description: Imaging through scattering media under natural illumination is a critical challenge in applications such as autonomous driving, underwater detection, and aviation safety. We propose a novel multi-view multi-step deconvolution image enhancement method for imaging in scattering media, which combines image background noise suppression, detail enhancement, and super-resolution. It integrates the advantages of dark channel prior preprocessing and adaptive deconvolution. A camera array-based common field of view multi-frame imaging model is established to address the challenges of long acquisition time and difficulty in registering moving objects in single-camera systems, thereby enabling multi-frame image acquisition of objects. Then, a multi-step adaptive deconvolution scattering imaging algorithm is designed for image enhancement, background noise suppression, and super-resolution imaging of objects, enabling multi-frame image fusion. The proposed approach tackles the issue of low signal-to-noise ratio in scattering environments, achieving high-quality passive imaging in complex scattering scenes. Finally, the proposed method is validated through simulations, laboratory-equivalent experiments, and real-world experiments, demonstrating its ability to achieve high-quality imaging in complex scattering scenarios with natural illumination, especially in heavy haze environments with limited camera quantities. The results show that when comparing with the existing methods under artificial heavy, moderate, and light haze conditions, the proposed method improves PSNR by 2.24%, 1.83%, and 2.95%, and SSIM by 10.93%, 5.21%, and 5.53%, respectively.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Computational Imaging - new TOC

item.title: MDbFusion++: A Visible and Infrared Image Fusion Framework Capable for Motion Deblurring
item.description: Existing image fusion methods focus on containing more complementary information, but source images always suffer from motion blur owing to object motion, which results in distorted details in fused images and further deteriorates performance on high-level tasks. This paper proposes a novel visible and infrared image fusion framework capable for motion deblurring (MDbFusion++), which can simultaneously perform image fusion and deblurring within a mutually reinforcing framework. MDbFusion++ employs a coarse-to-fine image restoration strategy and comprises two key components: a coarse deblurring part (CDP) and a fine deblurring and fusion part (FDFP). Firstly, CDP transfers multi-modal images into features corresponding to spatial locations and creatively leverages infrared features to coarsely compensate motion blurred visible ones through adaptive weights module (AWM). Subsequently, FDFP further restores fine visible features and achieves multi-modal images fusion in spatial and frequency domains with the help of multi-domain enhancement module (MEM). The deblurred visible features provide clear information to improve fusion results, and the improved fused images, in turn, provide gradient feedback to further improve deblurring effects. We evaluate our network in terms of both image deblurring and fusion, and extensive comparative experiments demonstrate the superior performance and distinct advantages of MDbFusion++.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation in Biomedical Images Segmentation With Guided Diffusion Generative Prior
item.description: Semantic segmentation has suffered for a while from a lack of datasets such as ImageNet for image classification. This issue was partially alleviated by the advent of the segment anything model (SAM), which provides a foundation model trained on the largest and most diverse segmentation dataset to date. However, the SAM often falls short in segmenting specific regions, mostly in regard to biomedical images; this is why unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) remains the best option for addressing the challenge of generalization capabilities. Classical UDA methods might be ineffective in several biomedical segmentation cases because the gap between two datasets, named domain shift, is too high. To address this issue, we propose a strategy based on learning the source mask probability distribution with a segmentation diffusion model as a generative prior to propose accurate target segmentation at inference. This latter can be guided by supplementary inputs, which allows us to call for the rich information contained in SAM raw segmentation both to perform adaptation and to improve robustness. A study was conducted using a comprehensive collection of segmentation datasets: 3 domains for mitochondria, 2 for the endoplasmic reticulum, and 2 for brain tumors, allowing the creation of 10 adaptation scenarios and providing an extensive test basis. The results of the experiments reveal that our proposed method outperforms various state-of-the-art UDA methods. Furthermore, ablation studies highlight the significant role of each component of our presented strategy. The code is available at: https://github.com/alex-stenger/GUDA
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing - new TOC

item.title: RAMPSNet: A Rotation-Aware Multigranularity Polarimetric Spatial Network for PolSAR Image Classification
item.description: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) image classification is a fundamental remote sensing interpretation, which remains challenging because polarimetric responses vary strongly with target orientation and radar incidence angles. These variations introduce complex backscattering properties that are difficult to model. To address this issue, we present the rotation-aware multigranularity polarimetric spatial network (RAMPSNet), which adopts a hierarchical and modular design to extract rotation-consistent features from both the PolSAR data rotation domain and multigranularity polarimetric spatial perspective. RAMPSNet consists of three core modules. Specifically, the Rotation-Aware Module (RAM) explicitly models dependencies along the rotation dimension by combining a learnable rotation positional embedding, a rotation-aware self-attention mechanism, and a lightweight feedforward network. These components jointly capture global and local rotational relationships and allow RAM to extract stable and rotation-consistent polarimetric features. The multigranularity feature extraction block (MGFEB) splits the input along the channel dimension into three parallel branches: a global frequency-aware branch, a local multikernel convolution branch, and an identity mapping branch. This design enables MGFEB to extract complementary structural, textural, and fine-grained polarimetric spatial features for richer semantic information learning with low computational overhead. Finally, the Channel–Spatial Interaction Module sequentially models channel and spatial dependencies to enhance discriminative features, suppress redundancy, and ensure structural coherence and produces discriminative final representations. Experiments on three widely used PolSAR datasets show that RAMPSNet consistently achieves higher accuracy than state-of-the-art methods, as well as fewer parameters and lower computational cost.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Refined Measurement of Equatorial Ionospheric Scintillation Using L-Band SAR Images
item.description: Amplitude stripes imposed by the ionospheric scintillation effects are widely present in the low-latitude nighttime acquisitions of L-band synthetic aperture radar (SAR) satellites, which is beneficial to space weather studies. In previous studies, the process of amplitude stripe extraction and spectral analysis was applied to equatorial ionospheric scintillation measurement based on those SAR images suffering visible amplitude stripes. However, existing extraction methods often struggle to cleanly separate the ionospheric signal from the underlying surface features. Consequently, measurements frequently become discontinuous or fail in certain areas, making manual data selection necessary and hindering automated processing. In this article, an adaptive directional multiscale filter (ADMF) based on the frequency-domain pattern of amplitude stripes is well designed to precisely extract the amplitude stripes from subband images. By comparison with the available two filters, the proposed filter proves to apply to different scenarios and stripes in different orientations, and the corresponding results of spectral analysis have higher robustness. Finally, an application case of the whole process for equatorial ionospheric scintillation measurement is perfectly examined in a group of ALOS PALSAR images that contains 75 consecutive scenarios and covers an azimuth distance of over 4100 km. Both 1-D and 2-D measurement results show refined distribution of the ionospheric scintillation parameters for the complete scenario, and the sampling resolution along-track reaches a few pixels (tens of meters), which indicates tremendous potential in automatic measurement for thousands of L-band SAR images suffering visible amplitude stripes.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Toward Stable Semi-Supervised Remote Sensing Segmentation via Co-Guidance and Co-Fusion
item.description: Semi-supervised remote sensing (RS) image semantic segmentation offers a promising solution to alleviate the burden of exhaustive annotation, yet it fundamentally struggles with pseudo-label drift, a phenomenon where confirmation bias leads to the accumulation of errors during training. In this work, we propose Co2S, a stable semi-supervised RS segmentation framework that synergistically fuses priors from vision-language models (VLMs) and self-supervised models (SSMs). Specifically, we construct a heterogeneous dual-student architecture comprising two distinct ViT-based vision foundation models initialized with the weights of pretrained CLIP and DINOv3 to mitigate error accumulation and pseudo-label drift. To effectively incorporate these distinct priors, a semantic co-guidance mechanism is introduced that utilizes text embeddings and learnable queries to provide explicit and implicit class-level guidance, respectively, thereby jointly enhancing semantic consistency. Furthermore, a global–local feature collaborative fusion strategy is developed to effectively fuse the global contextual information captured by CLIP with the local details produced by DINOv3, enabling the model to generate highly precise segmentation results. Extensive experiments on six popular datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, which consistently achieves leading performance across various partition protocols and diverse scenarios. Project page is available at https://xavierjiezou.github.io/Co2S/
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: DeH4R: A Decoupled and Hybrid Method for Road Network Graph Extraction
item.description: The automated extraction of complete and precise road network graphs from remote sensing imagery remains a critical challenge in geospatial computer vision. Segmentation-based approaches, while effective in pixel-level recognition, struggle to maintain topology fidelity after vectorization postprocessing. Graph-growing methods build more topologically faithful graphs but suffer from computationally prohibitive iterative region of interest (ROI) cropping. Graph-generating methods first predict global static candidate road network vertices, and then infer possible edges between vertices. They achieve fast topology-aware inference, but limit the dynamic insertion of vertices. To address these challenges, we propose DeH4R, a novel hybrid model that combines graph-generating efficiency and graph-growing dynamics. This is achieved by decoupling the task into candidate vertex detection, adjacent vertex prediction, initial graph construction, and graph expansion. This architectural innovation enables dynamic vertex (edge) insertions while retaining fast inference speed and enhancing both topology fidelity and spatial consistency. Furthermore, it is simple and straightforward to implement. Comprehensive evaluations on the CityScale and SpaceNet benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance. DeH4R outperforms the prior SOTA graph-growing method RNGDet++ by 4.66 average path length similarity (APLS) and 10.18 intersection over union (IoU) on CityScale, while being approximately $10\times $ faster. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/7777777FAN/DeH4R
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Lightweight ORSI Salient Object Detection via Frequency and Mutual Assistance Attention
item.description: Lightweight Salient Object Detection in Optical Remote Sensing Image (ORSI-SOD) is expected to achieve a good balance between model complexity and detection accuracy. Existing lightweight ORSI-SOD methods usually adopt the MobileNet as the backbone, which greatly reduces the model complexity but also restricts the detection accuracy. In this article, we propose a novel lightweight Frequency and $M$ utual Assistance Attention Network, i.e., FreMaNet, with a lightweight transformer backbone for ORSI-SOD. Our FreMaNet is built on the strategy of intralevel modeling and interlevel assistance. Frequency-domain self-attention (FreSA) and mutual assistance channel attention (MaCA) are responsible for intralevel modeling and interlevel assistance, respectively. Specifically, FreSA is arranged behind the backbone to further model global relationships within each level of features (i.e., intralevel features). Different from the vanilla self-attention, FreSA achieves global relationship modeling through multiplication in the frequency domain, resulting in less computational load. Then, different levels of features (i.e., interlevel features) assist and interact with each other in MaCA. MaCA first performs a simple fusion on the features of two adjacent levels and then adopts parallel self-channel attention (SelfCA) and assistance channel attention to adaptively achieve mutual assistance of features at different levels. With the cooperation of the above components and an efficient saliency decoder, our FreMaNet has only 4.91M parameters and 4.52G floating-point operations (FLOPs) for a $352\times 352$ input. Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate that our lightweight FreMaNet achieves competitive performance compared to lightweight and normal-sized ORSI-SOD methods. The code and results of our method are available at https://github.com/MathLee/FreMaNet
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: NASTaR: A NovaSAR-Based Automated Ship Target Recognition Dataset
item.description: We introduce the NovaSAR Automated Ship Target Recognition (NASTaR) dataset. This dataset comprises 3415 ship patches extracted from NovaSAR S-band imagery, with labels matched to AIS data. It includes distinctive features such as 23 unique classes, inshore/offshore separation, and an auxiliary wake dataset for patches where ship wakes are visible. We validated the dataset’s applicability across prominent ship-type classification scenarios using benchmark deep learning (DL) models. Results demonstrate over 60% accuracy for classifying four major ship types, over 70% for a three-class scenario, more than 75% for distinguishing cargo from tanker ships, and over 87% for identifying fishing vessels. The NASTaR dataset is available at https://doi.org/10.5523/bris.2tfa6x37oerz2lyiw6hp47058, while relevant codes for benchmarking and analysis are available at https://github.com/benyaminhosseiny/nastar
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: PsSAR-v1.0: Unlocking SAR Panoptic Segmentation
item.description: Panoptic segmentation technology can simultaneously acquire information about objects and background, and thus holds extremely high application value in remote sensing image interpretation. However, the development of this technology in the field of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) information processing is limited, with the core issue lying in the lack of high-quality public datasets. To address this gap, this study constructs the first SAR panoptic segmentation dataset, PsSAR-v1.0. Based on the high-resolution SAR ship dataset, this dataset explores and proposes a standard workflow for label production of panoptic segmentation datasets, and realizes the joint annotation of ship targets and sea–land backgrounds for the first time. With excellent compatibility, the dataset can be adapted to open source panoptic segmentation toolboxes (e.g., mmdetection and detectron2). The dataset contains 5604 sets of images and their corresponding panoptic segmentation annotations, with the total number of annotated pixels reaching $3.6\times 10^{9}$ . In addition, this letter establishes a performance benchmark based on this dataset, providing a reliable baseline reference for the academic community. This research effectively breaks through the bottleneck of data scarcity and is expected to further promote the development of SAR panoptic segmentation technology. The dataset and baseline codes are available at: https://github.com/PerSARption/PsSAR-v1.0-Unlocking-SAR-Panoptic-Segmentation
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: Robust Phase Error Reconstruction for Airborne Stripmap SAR via Global Optimization
item.description: Accurate phase error compensation plays a critical role in high-resolution imaging in airborne stripmap synthetic aperture radar (SAR). Traditional stripmap autofocus approaches, such as phase curvature autofocus (PCAs), typically rely on a fragile chain-like strategy to reconstruct phase errors. The fundamental flaw is that a single local estimation failure, often triggered by radio frequency interference (RFI), easily propagates along the chain, thus corrupting the entire aperture. To overcome this vulnerability, this letter proposes a robust reconstruction framework based on global network optimization. We transform the problem into a globally connected, overdetermined, sparse linear system. By exploiting the constraint among all overlapping subapertures, the proposed method creates redundant connectivity paths that effectively mitigate the impact of local outliers and prevent the propagation of local estimation errors. The simulations and experimental results show that, compared with traditional PCA, the proposed algorithm exhibits stronger robustness and improved focusing performance.
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: FFDMamba: Feature Frequency Decoupling Mamba for High-Resolution Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
item.description: High-resolution remote sensing image segmentation is crucial for applications such as urban planning, disaster management and environmental monitoring. However, achieving high accuracy remains challenging due to the need to understand the broader context while also preserving fine-grained detail. While convolutional neural networks (CNNs) excel at capturing local details, they struggle with global context. The emerging Mamba (visual state space model) architecture is better suited to modeling long-range dependencies due to its linear complexity; however, it tends to accentuate low-frequency components, which could compromise edges and textures. To maximize the synergistic effects between CNNs and Mamba, we therefore propose a novel framework called feature frequency decoupling Mamba (FFDMamba). This method integrates two components: a feature frequency decoupling module (FFDM) and an improved parallel attention gate (PAG) module. The FFDM module separates features into distinct frequency bands, enabling the network to effectively model low-frequency contextual information with Mamba while preserving high-frequency details. Meanwhile, the improved PAG module refines the encoder–decoder skip connections via pixel-level attention gating, selectively emphasising important features to enhance multiscale feature fusion and reduce the loss of spatial details during decoding. Experimental results on high-resolution remote sensing datasets demonstrate that FFDMamba achieves a superior tradeoff between segmentation accuracy and computational complexity. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our approach delivers significant performance gains with manageable resource overhead, confirming its effectiveness and practical value for high-precision remote sensing interpretation.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: AsymSAM: Asymmetric Feature-Enhanced SAM for Remote Sensing Polygonal Buildings Mapping
item.description: Accurate polygonal building extraction requires not only region-level segmentation but also geometrically consistent vertex modeling. However, existing segment anything model (SAM)-based adaptations primarily optimize mask quality and lack explicit mechanisms for structured polygon prediction, leading to irregular contours and unstable vertex localization, especially under cross-domain scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose AsymSAM, an asymmetric feature-enhanced framework based on the SAM for polygonal building extraction. AsymSAM integrates an asymmetric hierarchical feature enhancement scheme and a hybrid fusion module in vanilla SAM. These components effectively leverage multilevel spatial and semantic information to enhance the representation of building contours and structural cues. Additionally, we extend the mask decoder to support joint prediction of polygon vertices and boundaries, enabling both automatic and prompt-guided extraction modes. Experiments on the WHU-Mix and SpaceNet datasets show AsymSAM consistently outperforms existing methods in terms of average precision (AP), average recall (AR), and intersection over union (IoU).
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: DFE-Net: A Discriminative Feature Enhancement Network for Aircraft Detection and Recognition Based on Spaceborne SAR Complex-Valued Images
item.description: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) aircraft detection and recognition play a crucial role in tasks, such as flight scheduling and analysis of airport traffic. However, SAR images suffer from inherent sidelobe interference and speckle noise. These factors degrade the integrity of aircraft scattering features, thereby limiting the accuracy of fine-grained detection and recognition. To address these challenges, a discriminative feature enhancement network (DFE-Net) is proposed. The key lies in constructing a discriminative feature from SAR complex-valued images, where scattering invariants and characteristic variables are effectively decoupled and enhanced. A discriminative channel optimization (DCO) module is proposed to obtain a trichannel representation for sidelobe and speckle suppression as well as target feature enhancement. DCO learns trichannel interactions and adaptively fuses shared and specific cues. This mechanism expands interclass separability and provides a robust feature space for fine-grained tasks. Furthermore, a contour-prior guidance module fuses contour priors with deep features. This highlighting of critical contours enhances geometric consistency while reducing false alarms. By delivering stable and semantically enriched features, DFE-Net significantly improves detection and recognition performance under limited image quality. Extensive experiments on the SAR-CARD dataset from Gaofen-3 demonstrate that DFE-Net consistently outperforms existing baselines in terms of both accuracy and robustness.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: MFTriNet: A Multimodal Fusion Triple-Flow Network for Remote Sensing Image Semantic Segmentation
item.description: The fusion of high-resolution remote sensing imagery (HRRSI) with digital surface models (DSM) provides complementary spectral and height information, significantly enhancing the potential for accurate semantic segmentation. However, effectively reconciling the inherent domain disparities between these modalities and mitigating noise from defective data sources remain challenging. While prevailing encoder–decoder networks facilitate cross-modal fusion, their performance often degrades in complex scenes due to limited robustness to intermodal discrepancies and noise. To address these challenges, we propose MFTriNet, a novel multimodal fusion triple-flow network that jointly considers modality-specific characteristics and cross-modal interactions. MFTriNet decomposes the segmentation task into two modality-specific flows and one modality-complementary flow. The two modality-specific flows, sharing identical encoder–decoder architectures, independently process HRRSI and DSM data to generate preliminary segmentation predictions and preserve unique modal characteristics. In the modality-complementary flow, each encoding layer incorporates a dedicated modality-induced feature modulator designed for feature alignment and discrepancy suppression, utilizing a strategy of correction followed by fusion, and each decoding layer integrates a modality-aware dynamic aggregation module for dynamic and gated attention-based fusion. The final prediction is generated by a learnable weighted fusion of outputs from all three flows. Extensive experiments on the ISPRS Vaihingen and Potsdam benchmarks demonstrate that MFTriNet achieves state-of-the-art performance, with mean intersection over union/overall accuracy values of 84.21%/92.05% and 86.78%/91.78%, respectively. Furthermore, evaluations on the more challenging MMHunan dataset verify the effectiveness and generalization capability of MFTriNet in complex scenes.
item.dc:source: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Graph-Modulated Attention and Centrality-Enhanced Network for Remote Sensing Object Counting
item.description: Object counting in remote sensing images remains highly challenging due to the dense distribution and high similarity of targets. Existing Transformer-based counting models have achieved remarkable success, but they often suffer from the attention map response convergence issue, where attention maps across different regions become overly similar. To address this limitation, we propose graph-modulated attention and centrality-enhanced network (GACENet), a novel architecture that rethinks attention mechanisms through the lens of graph-structured diversity enhancement. Our framework introduces three groundbreaking components: the anti-similarity attention graph (ASAG) module, the centrality-aware feature enhancement (CAFE) mechanism, and a composite loss function. First, the ASAG module dynamically learns a graph that explicitly models semantic dissimilarity between regions, forcing attention heads to focus on complementary features rather than converging to redundant patterns. Second, the CAFE module is designed to mitigate weak responses to key target regions caused by attention homogeneity. It is a topological prior-driven module that identifies and amplifies critical nodes via graph centrality analysis, ensuring robust representation of key targets even under extreme density conditions. Finally, a composite loss function that combines a structural loss (SL) and a diversity loss, which encourages response variation across spatial locations. Extensive experiments on multiple remote sensing counting datasets demonstrate that GACENet significantly outperforms other state-of-the-art remote counting methods in terms of mean absolute error (MAE) and mean squared error (mse).
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: Layout-Controlled Synthetic Data Generation for Remote Sensing Object Detection
item.description: High-performance remote sensing object detection typically requires vast datasets with annotations, but the collection and labeling of large-scale data is a costly and labor-intensive process. While generative models have shown promise in addressing the need for large-scale datasets, they heavily rely on high-quality labeled data and often overlook crucial aspects, such as the interobject relationships and overall quality of the synthetic data. To this end, we propose layout-controlled synthetic data generation (LSDGen), a novel approach for generating high-fidelity remote sensing imagery for object detection. LSDGen consists of three key stages: 1) relation-constrained layout generation (RLGen), which constructs a category association matrix from benchmark data to generate both main and subcategories, as well as their spatial configurations, without relying on bounding-box annotations; 2) image synthesis via a layout-conditional diffusion model (LCDM), where the generated layout guides a diffusion model to produce high-quality remote sensing images; and 3) instance-centered synthetic data filtering (ISDF), which evaluates the semantic consistency of individual instances in the synthesized images by calculating instance-level cosine similarity scores between visual and textual representations, filtering out low-confidence samples based on the average semantic score. Experimental results on the object DetectIon in Optical Remote sensing images (DIOR), oriented object detection benchmark based on DIOR dataset (DIOR-R), and high-resolution ship corpus (HRSC) datasets demonstrate that our method significantly improves the detection performance of existing remote sensing image object detection models, outperforming state-of-the-art annotation-dependent synthesis approaches.
item.dc:source: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing - new TOC

item.title: EfficientBGD-SAM: A Boundary-Gated Decoder for Lake-Ice Segmentation
item.description: Lake-ice maps are essential for safety and climate applications, yet automated mapping remains challenging due to diffuse boundaries and large data volumes. We propose EfficientBGD-SAM, a boundary-aware encoder–decoder that produces reliable lake-ice masks at operational scale. The model introduces internally supervised spatial gates on decoder skip connections: a narrow boundary band supervises the gates and weights the prediction head to emphasize boundary-critical pixels during training. In addition, lightweight channel attention on skip features suppresses clutter. EfficientBGD-SAM surpasses state-of-the-art methods, achieving Dice 0.9461, IoU 0.9004, and bIoU 0.8784, while running in 3.96 ms with 14.83 GFLOPs. Ablations show that attention-only skips are insufficient; the learned gating provides the primary improvement while preserving region accuracy. These results provide faster processing and support near real-time lake-ice monitoring for operational planning and environmental decision-making.
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: AnisoColSAR: A Physics-Informed Approach for SAR Image Colorization by Encoding Scattering Anisotropy
item.description: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) products are mostly presented as grayscale images, where the scattering characteristics of ground objects lack intuitive visualization, limiting the performance of downstream interpretation tasks. To address this issue, this letter proposes AnisoColSAR, a physics-informed SAR image colorization method. Based on the physical scattering mechanism of ground objects, AnisoColSAR takes the scattering anisotropy index (SAI) as a proxy for physical anisotropy. By quantifying and encoding the SAI into color features, it generates pseudo-color images with explicit physical significance without full-polarization or multisource auxiliary data. Experiments on the AIR-PolSAR-Seg-2.0 dataset demonstrate that the proposed pseudo-color images stably improve semantic segmentation performance across all polarization modes (HH, VV, HV, VH), with particularly significant gains in cross-polarization modes. This study provides a novel physics-guided approach for SAR image interpretation, and the generated pseudo-color images are promising for various remote sensing tasks such as classification and detection, showing strong practical value.
item.dc:source: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters - new TOC

item.title: Segmental Diffusion: A Distance-Guided Diffusion for Time Series Forecasting
item.description: Diffusion models have recently shown promise for time series forecasting (TSF), but existing methods rely solely on reverse denoising from Gaussian noise, introducing uncertainty and disrupting temporal structures. We propose Segmental Diffusion (SegDiff), a distance-aware framework for TSF that retrieves semantically similar historical segments to guide each diffusion step via an index-guided conditioning mechanism.
